ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for tandoori chicken salad specifically; mapped to broader 'chicken dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the primary ingredient anchor for tandoori murgh salad; salad composite not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for tandoori chicken salad. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is used as the closest parent ingredient anchor. Salad vegetables and dressing components are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps to tandoori chicken salad at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tandoori Murgh Salad is approximately 250 g, comprising a portion of tandoori-grilled boneless chicken placed on a bed of fresh lettuce tossed with house dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Murghsalad (Chef Recommendation) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tandoori Murghsalad (Chef Recommendation), cr_id G00010848. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 05165, Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; the estimate of 3.5g/100g reflects the fat fraction attributable to saturated fatty acids in a high-fat dressing-dominant dish. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat are set to -1 as HPB does not report these for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed salad with protein and dressing (GI ~32, analogous to low-GI mixed salad with protein)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tandoori Murgh Salad. GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed green salad with protein and oil-based dressing, which is a low-GI food category. The very low carbohydrate content (5.9g/100g) and high fat content suppress postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (32 × 5.9 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.7, confirming low GL. This is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may vary with dressing composition.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request dressing on the side, ask for reduced salt in the marinade, and limit portion to no more than 100 g to keep sodium intake below 810 mg per occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 5.9 g/100g and sugar 4.69 g/100g are low, supporting glycaemic control, but fat at 44.54 g/100g is very high and may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content is favourable for blood glucose management, but the very high fat content (44.54 g/100g) can impair insulin sensitivity over time; request low-fat dressing and limit portion size to 150–200 g, pairing with high-fibre vegetables to compensate for zero dietary fibre.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 44.54 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~3.5 g/100g, combined with sodium 810 mg/100g, presents compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of very high total fat, elevated sodium, and cholesterol (26 mg/100g) makes this dish unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with cardiovascular disease; if consumed occasionally, request grilled chicken without heavy dressing and limit portion to 100 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g exceeds the RA threshold of 400 mg/100g; zero dietary fibre provides no anti-inflammatory microbiome benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sodium at 810 mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling and inflammation; request reduced salt in marinade and dressing, avoid nightshade accompaniments such as tomatoes and bell peppers, and pair with anti-inflammatory omega-3-rich foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Murgh Salad (Chef Recommendation) is an Indian-inspired restaurant dish served in Singapore, consisting of boneless chicken marinated in a spiced yogurt tandoori blend and grilled in a tandoor oven, then placed on a bed of fresh lettuce tossed with a house dressing. The dish delivers the characteristic smoky, charred flavour of tandoori cooking with the freshness of crisp salad greens. The HPB Singapore Foodo database records this dish at 430 kcal per 100g, driven primarily by a very high fat content of 44.54 g/100g, which is consistent with a dressing-dominant preparation where the house dressing contributes the majority of caloric density. Protein is unusually low at 1.32 g/100g for a chicken dish, suggesting the nutritional profile may reflect a dressing-heavy portion or a small chicken-to-salad ratio. Sodium is critically high at 810 mg/100g, exceeding safe thresholds for hypertension and rheumatoid arthritis. Carbohydrates are low at 5.9 g/100g with sugar at 4.69 g/100g, making the glycaemic impact minimal. Dietary fibre is zero, which limits its anti-inflammatory and gut health benefits. This dish is classified as high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and fat load, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the fat-driven insulin sensitivity impact despite low carbohydrates. It is suitable as an occasional meal for healthy adults but requires dressing modification and portion control for individuals with chronic conditions.
common_user_queries: Is tandoori chicken salad good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010848, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this is documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this dish.
notes: 1. Protein at 1.32 g/100g is unusually low for a chicken-based dish; this likely reflects a dressing-dominant portion composition where the house dressing contributes the majority of mass and calories. Full dish composition verification is recommended. 2. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB database entry; this is plausible for a salad with dressing-dominant composition and minimal vegetable fibre contribution as recorded. 3. Saturated_fat_g (3.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05165, cooked chicken meat) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry; this is a conservative estimate. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry, and no approved secondary source provides these values for this specific composite dish. 5. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on mixed green salad with protein and oil-based dressing; actual GI may vary with dressing composition. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the Indian Muslim restaurant context typical for tandoori dishes in Singapore; formal halal certification should be verified with the specific restaurant.
